coredata.dc:title: High-performance perovskite solar cells using the graphene quantum dot–modified SnO2/ZnO photoelectrode
coredata.dc:creator: Nagaraj, G.
Mohammed, Mustafa K.A.
Shekargoftar, Masoud
Sasikumar, P.
Sakthivel, P.
Ravi, G.
Dehghanipour, M.
Akin, Seckin
Shalan, Ahmed Esmail
coredata.dc:description: Regardless of the excellent improvement in the assembling of perovskite solar cells (PSCs), the photon-harvesting performance of these devices is inadequate through the disproportionate recombination of generated charge carriers. The improvement of the charge carrier mobility can significantly reduce the recombination and help the perovskite devices reach the theoretical power conversion efficiency (PCE). The modification of charge selective contacts is one of the most effective approaches for reducing the carrier recombination. Herein, we introduce a facile and effective doping engineering approach based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the modification of the SnO2/ZnO bilayer electron transport layer (ETL). A comparative study of perovskite films deposited on SnO2/ZnO layers with altered concentrations of GQDs was employed to significantly enhance the opto-electronic properties. The integration of GQDs into the ETL indicates a potential for improving the charge carrier transporting in PSCs. Overall, the PSC using the 4% GQD-modified ETL yields a PCE of 19.81% with a striking open-circuit voltage (V OC) of 1.17 V. Besides, 4% GQD-modified ETL-based devices enhance the long-term ambient and thermal stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskites
Electron transport layer
ZnO/SnO2
Graphene quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Configuration of the PSC used in this study: glass/FTO/GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO/Cs0.05(FA0.95MA0.05)0.95Pb(I0.95Br0.05)3/BABr/spiro-OMeTAD/Au. (b) The diagram of energy levels of each layer of the corresponding PSC.

Photovoltaic measurements. (a) J–V curves of PSCs based on pristine SnO2 and bilayer SnO2/ZnO ETLs. (b) J–V curves of PSCs based on GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO ETLs. (c) IPCE spectra of PSCs with 0% and 4% GQDs. (d) Hysteresis effect of corresponding PSCs.

Perovskite film measurements. AFM images of perovskite films deposited on (a) pristine SnO2, (b) optimized SnO2/ZnO, and (c) 4% GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO ETLs. (d) XRD patterns and (e) Absorption spectra of corresponding films.

(a) Steady-state PL spectra. (b) TRPL spectra. (c) Nyquist plots. Inset: The fitted equivalent circuit. (d) The SCLC measurement of the electron-only devices based on the FTO/SnO2/ZnO/perovskite/PCBM/Au structure.

The stability test of unencapsulated PSCs with and without GQDs. (a) Stored in ambient air with an RH of 30–40%. (b) Thermal stability of PSCs kept in ambient air at 80 °C in the dark conditions. RH, relative humidity.
PV parameters of the PSCs based on SnO2/ZnO ETLs.
PV parameters of the PSCs based on GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO ETLs based on 6 nm ZnO.
Summary of related studies with the bilayer ETL and comparison with our present study.

row.entry: ≈18% loss after 56 days of the test in 30–40% RH in ambient air without encapsulation.

head.ce:title: High-performance perovskite solar cells using the graphene quantum dot–modified SnO2/ZnO photoelectrode

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Regardless of the excellent improvement in the assembling of perovskite solar cells (PSCs), the photon-harvesting performance of these devices is inadequate through the disproportionate recombination of generated charge carriers. The improvement of the charge carrier mobility can significantly reduce the recombination and help the perovskite devices reach the theoretical power conversion efficiency (PCE). The modification of charge selective contacts is one of the most effective approaches for reducing the carrier recombination. Herein, we introduce a facile and effective doping engineering approach based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the modification of the SnO2/ZnO bilayer electron transport layer (ETL). A comparative study of perovskite films deposited on SnO2/ZnO layers with altered concentrations of GQDs was employed to significantly enhance the opto-electronic properties. The integration of GQDs into the ETL indicates a potential for improving the charge carrier transporting in PSCs. Overall, the PSC using the 4% GQD-modified ETL yields a PCE of 19.81% with a striking open-circuit voltage (V OC) of 1.17 V. Besides, 4% GQD-modified ETL-based devices enhance the long-term ambient and thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Based on the auspicious features of the organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite materials, including their tremendous optical and electronic features, for instance, broad spectral absorption, long charge lifespan, intrinsic low trap density, and solution processability, they are considered as a talented applicant for photovoltaic (PV) applications [1–8]. The power conversion efficiency (PCE) of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) has augmented intensely from 3% to over 25.5% [9–11]. It was noted that the thermally unstable and volatile nature of the organic cations in the perovskite structure, despite their excellent results, is a major anxiety for large-scale commercialization of perovskite PVs [12,13]. Different attempts have been applied to minimize the energy loss in the assembled cells including adapted crystallization, surface passivation, doping, and architecture engineering [14,15]. Incorporating two-dimensional (2D) layered perovskites into three-dimensional (3D) hybrid perovskites is a viable strategy for enhancing stability while maintaining high performance [16,17]. The 2D fragment appears to act as an impurity, obstructing electron transport and increasing electron recombination. Inorganic cesium (Cs+) perovskites have acquired tremendous attention because of their increased thermal stability features [18]. The addition of formamidinium (FA) and/or methylammonium (MA) to inorganic CsPbX3-based (X = I−, Br−, Cl−) perovskite absorbers can progress crystallinity and the stability of the fabricated layers [19–21]. Recently, a 2D/3D heterostructure with butylammonium incorporation in the cesium-formamidinium lead halide perovskite is introduced to improve the PCE and increase the stability of the fabricated devices [22].

The creation of suitable charge-selective contacts, along with engineering the perovskite layer, is considered another important approach in defining system features for the reason that photoinduced carriers must move through the interface before reaching the electrodes. Numerous n-type metal oxide materials may be applied as an electron transport layer (ETL) in PSCs, with zinc oxide (ZnO), titanium dioxide (TiO2), tin dioxide (SnO2), and tungsten trioxide (WO3). Academic studies on TiO2 and ZnO ETLs for perovskite PVs have been widely published [23,24]. Aside from the traditional ETLs, a lot of work has gone into forming novel ETL materials [25–27]. For instance, Seok et al. [28] reached a PCE above 21% in PSCs by using BaSnO3 as an ETL, but V OC remained at only 1.12 V. Very recently, planar PSCs with the efficiency of 19.3% have been reported with zinc stannate (Zn2SnO4) ETL material, presenting a V OC of only 1.09 V [27]. The quality of the Zn2SnO4 layer is enhanced via a chemical bath after treatment. The treated ETL promoted a high PCE of 21% because of suppressed defects and a conformal development of perovskite. Furthermore, graphene-based materials are known as the game changer in perovskite PVs, in particular, may gain conductivity and align the band position when used as a dopant or interlayer in different configurations of PSCs [24].

Herein, we fabricated PSCs based on mixed Cs0.05(FA0.95MA0.05)0.95Pb(I0.95Br0.05)3/BABr 3D/2D perovskite and using the SnO2/ZnO bilayer film modified with graphene quantum dot (GQD) additives. These 3D/2D perovskite material fabrication methodologies provide a humble and collaborative setting for effective as well as air-stable perovskite film and system assembling. The incorporation of different concentrations of GQD (2%, 4%, and 6%) additives to the ETLs displayed a role in reducing the trapped-defect density, improving the carrier lifetime, and increasing the built-in potential, resulting in a high-quality ETL and helping in modifying the ETL/perovskite interface to form high-efficiency PSC with outstanding stability. The acquired results demonstrate that the devices with 4% GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO bilayer ETLs show the best PV parameters as PSCs reveal a V OC of 1.172 V, a J SC of 22.85 mA/cm2, an fill factor (FF) of 74%, and a PCE of 19.81%. Moreover, the corresponding devices retained ∼82% of their original performance after aging in ambient conditions for 56 days.

The schematic structure and energy level diagram of fabricated PSCs are demonstrated in Fig. 1 . The configuration of the fabricated device in this study is as follows: glass/F-doped tin oxide (FTO)/GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO ETL/perovskite/BABr/spiro-OMeTAD/Au (Fig. 1a). Moreover, the schematic of the layer-by-layer assembled device process is revealed in Fig. S1, in the supporting information (SI). Besides, the potential energy level diagram of each layer in the cell, as well as the possible transport of photo-generated carriers, electrons (e−), and holes (h+), is shown in Fig. 1b. The carrier separation in the perovskite film is found to be possible through a good match between band energies [29].

The PV features in addition to the stability of the mixed perovskite/BABr-based PSCs were improved through modification of the SnO2/ZnO bilayer film with GQD additives. To this end, first, the impact of the thickness of the ZnO interlayer on PSC performance was investigated to optimize our proposed strategy. After that, mixed 3D/2D PSCs were synthesized via a two-step sequential deposition approach (see the Experimental part in the SI). Furthermore, GQDs have a size of <5 nm in each dimension, indicating that they are zero-dimensional material, in accordance with the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) image (Figs. S2a and SI). Besides, the GQDs are evenly spaced and have normal spherical dots. The Raman spectrum of the GQDs is also shown in Figs. S2b and SI, with the Raman bands (the D-band at 1335.2 cm−1 and the G-band at 1582.4 cm−1) indicating the crystal nature of the prepared GQDs [1]. In addition, GQDs show a wide photoluminescence (PL) peak at 501 nm, as shown in Figs. S1c and SI. The absorption pattern of GQDs (Figs. S2d and SI) shows two bands at 245 and 332 nm, which may be due to an electronic transition of GQDs [30]. Evidently, the desired GQDs have been synthesized with suitable features that allow them to be used as dopant materials for the SnO2/ZnO bilayer.

After the fabrication of PSCs and the premonitory studies for the fabricated films, we have checked the PV parameters of assembled PSCs based on SnO2/ZnO ETLs and GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO ETLs for comparison. All the PV parameters, including J SC, V OC, FF, and PCE, are listed in Table 1 and Table 2 for devices with SnO2/ZnO layers and GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO ETLs, respectively. Fig. 2 a displays the J–V curve of the fabricated PSCs based on pristine SnO2 as well as SnO2/ZnO ETLs with different thicknesses of the ZnO layer (3 nm, 6 nm, and 9 nm), which were deposited through atomic layer deposition (ALD) technique. The obtained results indicated that the addition of the ZnO thin film on the SnO2 ETL (SnO2/ZnO bilayer) can improve the overall efficiency of the fabricated devices. The efficiency of PSCs with SnO2/ZnO ETLs with 6 nm thickness of ZnO was obtained to be 17.50%, which is higher than the PCE of the PSCs with SnO2-only ETLs (15.01%) (Table 1). This improvement will be further proved by systematic measurements; we show that the SnO2/ZnO bilayer has an improved carrier injection capability and reduced charge recombination at the interfaces, both of which are beneficial to photoinduced charge separation and transfer. After that, the fabricated PSCs with 6 nm of the ZnO layer in SnO2/ZnO bilayer ETL configuration were further considered the control device, and the impact of the GQDs on the PV merits of devices was examined as displayed in Fig. 2b. For that purpose, different concentrations of GQDs (2%, 4%, and 6%) were added to the SnO2/ZnO bilayer–based ETL. The acquired results demonstrate that devices with 4% GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO bilayer ETLs show the best PV parameters among the other dopant ratios as the devices show V OC of 1.172 V, J SC of 22.85 mA/cm2, FF of 74%, and PCE of 19.81% (Fig. 2b and Table 2). To explain the reduction of PV performance at high GQD ratio, the development of perovskite can be divided into two steps: nucleation and crystal growth. GQDs can act as heterogeneous nucleation sites of perovskite. Crystals tend to develop independently because of massive nucleation centers, resulting in more crystals with smaller sized grains. Too many GQDs caused aggregation, producing more defects inside the perovskite and generating a low-quality film [31]. Therefore, we determined the devices based on 4% GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO bilayer ETLs to be compared with the pristine cells for other characterizations. Furthermore, the related device statistical distribution, including V OC, J SC, FF, and PCE, of PSCs utilizing ZnO/SnO2 added with or without GQDs is illustrated in Fig. S3 (a-d), SI. As expected, the obtained PV parameters for the devices containing 4% of GQD modification are higher than that of the other devices. In more detail, PCE of 17.50% with J SC of 21.86 mA/cm2, V OC of 1.128 V, and FF of 71% was achieved for the pristine device, whereas a PCE of 19.81% with J SC of 22.85 mA/cm2, V OC of 1.172 V, and FF of 74% was achieved for GQD-modified devices.

Fig. 2c displays incident photo to charge carrier efficiency (IPCE) spectra and the integrated J SC values of devices based on ETLs with and without GQD modification. Compared with the PSCs without GQD modification, the treated PSCs demonstrate higher IPCE values, specifying the high photon-to-electron conversion ability in the corresponding devices. In addition, the integrated J SC of PSCs from the IPCE data reveals the same trend to the J SC from the experimental J–V plot and matches together. Furthermore, the hysteresis effect of fabricated devices was investigated by forward and reverse scans, which was found to be lower in PSCs without GQD modifications than in those with GQD modifications (Fig. 2d). The low hysteresis effect indicated for PSCs with GQD modification can be credited to better charge transfer and decrease in recombination as a result of higher conductivity and lower trap states in the GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO bilayer ETLs [32,33].

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was used to assess the possessions of diverse SnO2 layers on the morphology of the perovskite layers, Fig. 3 a–c. Besides, the perovskite layer deposited on the pristine SnO2 (Fig. 3a) showed small grains with many grain boundaries on the surface. The root mean square (RMS) values corresponding to the perovskite layer deposited on the pristine SnO2 were ∼15.0 nm. The AFM image (Fig. 3b and c) of the perovskite layer deposited on the SnO2/ZnO and 4% GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO ETL showed larger grains on the surface with RMS of ∼13.4 nm and ∼9.8 nm, respectively. Such a smooth film brings a more suitable contact between perovskite and hole transport layer (HTL) films, indicating the enhanced hole transport processes at the 3D/2D perovskite/HTL interfaces [21]. The grain boundaries disappeared for the perovskite layer prepared on the 4% GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO ETL. An optimized amount of GQDs provide a better crystallized perovskite film. The reason is that GQDs seize oxygen groups like OH and CO, which can produce strong bonds with the perovskite, ensuring a closer connection with perovskite crystals and better crystallization [1]. In Fig. 3d, we show the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra for a sequence of perovskite films deposited on different SnO2 layers. The results showed that the intensity of the characteristic perovskite (110) and (220) peaks increases as perovskite films deposited on the modified SnO2. The formation of perovskite crystallization contains two steps: nucleation and crystal growth, where GQDs can serve as the heterogeneous nucleation centers of perovskite. Perovskite crystals tend to grow better because of nucleation sites, leading to larger crystals with higher crystallization [34]. No shifts in the position of the peaks observed suggested that the Miller planes become increasingly aligned by deposition of perovskite films on the modified SnO2 layer [14]. Moreover, the X-ray spectra of the perovskite layers show an obvious signal at 5.18° in the low-angle region, which attributed the 2D perovskite phase and implying the formation of 2D/3D perovskite heterostructure [35]. UV–vis measurement further confirms that modified-SnO2 layers cannot affect the absorption edge of the perovskite layer (Fig. 3e). We observed a slight increase in the absorption spectra in the lower wavelength after modification of the SnO2 layer because of the boosted film quality, as stated in XRD and AFM results.

We carried out a series of spectroscopic techniques based on PL, time-resolved PL (TRPL), and impedance spectroscopy on the perovskite film deposited on the SnO2 layer to confirm the positive effect of GQD additives to the SnO2/ZnO bilayer. Fig. 4 a indicates that the SSPL intensity is significantly reduced for the perovskite layer deposited on 4% GQDs, suggesting a modification of the SnO2 layer to simplify the charge separation and decrease the charge recombination [4]. In addition, carrier lifetimes were calculated from TRPL (Fig. 4b), and the results are summarized in Tables S1 and SI. We fitted the spectra by using three components. The time constants for the sample corresponding to 0% GQDs are τ1 = 6.33 ns, τ2 = 26.46 ns, and τ3 = 145.62 ns and for the sample which used 4% GQDs are τ1 = 5.48 ns, τ2 = 21.49 ns, and τ3 = 135.68 ns. This calculation means that the GQD-free ETL resulted in a longer lifetime of the carriers than the 4% GQD-doped ETL. One can conclude that the perovskite layer deposited on the SnO2 with a lower concentration of the GQDs benefits the lifetime of the charge carriers. Impedance spectroscopy measurements were achieved to estimate the interfacial properties of the PSC system. Fig. 4c shows the Nyquist plots of PSCs corresponding to devices with SnO2/ZnO and the GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO bilayer. The plots consist of two semicircles and consider that the radius of these semicircles is proportional to the internal resistance at the interface of PSCs [36,37]. The recombination resistance (R rec) in the simulated equivalent circuit (inset of Fig. 4c) is related to the interfacial resistance of perovskite/charge transport layers. The fitted electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) values of PSCs based on pure SnO2/ZnO and GQD-doped SnO2/ZnO are summarized in Tables S2 and SI. The R rec of 0% GQDs was 1278 Ω and increased to1358 Ω for device corresponding to 4% GQDs (see Tables S2 and SI). The increased R rec indicates the reduced surface defects and thus manifested in the improved FF and V OC (as showed in Table 2). Furthermore, the measurement of the space-charge limited current (SCLC) method was performed to acquire more information related to the effects of modified SnO2 on the trap density of the perovskite layer. First, to find the trap-state density (Nt) of perovskites by SCLC measurements, we constructed the electron-only architecture as FTO/SnO2/ZnO/perovskite/PCBM/Au (Fig. 4d). As per the equation
V
T F L
=
e
N t
L 2
2 ε
ε o
, the Nt can be determined through the trap-filled limit voltage (V TFL) [38,39], where e, L, ε, and ε
o are the electron charge, the thickness of the perovskite layer, the relative dielectric constant, and the vacuum permittivity, respectively. The V TFL magnitudes of perovskites deposited on pristine and modified SnO2/ZnO are 0.82 V and 0.61 V, with a corresponding Nt of 7.78 × 1019 cm−3 and 5.78 × 1019 cm−3, respectively. The remarkably lower Nt confirms that the defects are reduced by doped GQDs, which may be related to better quality of perovskites [40,41].

To further compare the effect of GQDs, ETL-only structures with the design of FTO/SnO2/ZnO/Au were fabricated; the I–V tests of two devices were recorded and revealed in Fig. S4. It is seen that the GQD-doped bilayer shows improved electrical conductivity and lower resistance compared with the unmodified film. The findings suggest that the GQD-doped film is more favorable for electron transfer than the unmodified film [1]. The conductivity could be calculated via the formula reported in the previous study [42]. The electrical conductivities of the SnO2/ZnO only and GQD-doped films are 2.37 × 10−5 and 3.67 × 10−5 S/cm, respectively.

Moreover, we investigated the stability of the 3D/2D mixed devices based on the SnO2/ZnO bilayer with and without GQD additives. The PSCs without any encapsulation were maintained in the ambient environment with a relative humidity of 30–40%. Four representative PSCs were used from different batches of the respective triple-cation perovskite devices with J–V plots recorded at various time intervals. Fig. 5 a shows a set of normalized PCEs as a function of aging time. The GQD-modified PSCs revealed excellent PCE over the whole test period and retained 82% of the original PCE after 56 days, whereas the PCE of the untreated PSCs gradually reduced to 40%. Besides, the thermal stability of unencapsulated PSCs was assessed at 80 °C with ambient humidity (Fig. 5b). The GQD-treated devices have better thermal stability than other pristine devices and maintain 88% of the initial PCE after 30 days, whereas the pristine PSCs quickly dropped to 67% of its initial PCE. The enlarged grain size with passivated grain boundaries (GBs) caused by the GQD additive is the reason for stability improvements (See Fig. 3). When grain boundaries in a perovskite film are passivated, routes are blocked for moisture diffusion into the perovskite structure and keep the perovskite layer safe from degradation. This phenomenon leads to higher humidity resistance in the perovskite layer, whereas PbI2 content in a perovskite layer rapidly reacts with humidity and then opens the degrading ways of the perovskite layer [42].

It is worth noting the achieved findings revealed that the designing of PSCs using the GQD-modified SnO2/ZnO bilayer in ambient conditions could increase the PCE and stability of the mixed 2D/3D perovskite devices, which is a step forward in PSC commercialization. Based on the comparison of the results listed in Table 3 , it can be argued that the most beneficial method for GQD usage in PSCs is incorporating it into the bilayer film, as reported here.

We have investigated the effects of GQD additives to the SnO2/ZnO bilayer on the performance of the PSCs. After determining the optimal ZnO thickness using ALD technique, we evaluated the surface properties of the perovskite films before and after GQD modification. The addition of 4% GQDs to the SnO2/ZnO ETL improved the surface morphology as well as the crystallinity of the fabricated perovskite layers. PL intensity was significantly reduced for the perovskite layer deposited on the ETL with 4% GQD, suggesting the lower charge recombination. The improvements of the perovskite film were manifested in better PV parameters, where the PCE increased from 17.50% to 19.81% for the GQD-modified ETL-based device. From the stability point of view, it was observed that PSCs deposited on the GQD-modified ETL were stable over time, preserving 82% of its initial performance after 56 days in ambient conditions. This work introduces an effective method for reducing the carrier recombination by incorporating GQDs into the SnO2/ZnO ETL and thus a facile modification technique to fabricate PSCs close to the theoretical performance.
All authors contributed equally to this study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of eco-friendly graphene quantum dots for highly efficient perovskite solar cells
ACS Appl. Energy Mater.

Ceram. Int.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-term stable and hysteresis-free planar perovskite solar cells using green antisolvent strategy
